refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-006 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall generate and maintain a real-time worksite model representing terrain elevation, static obstacles (rocks, structures, vegetation), dynamic hazards (personnel, other equipment), and work-zone boundaries, with sufficient fidelity to support autonomous path planning and operator situational awareness.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on fidelity requirements, update frequency, and use-case binding. Refinement clarifies that the model must serve both planning (offline) and execution (real-time) functions, must distinguish static from dynamic features, and must be safety-critical (high criticality factor). This enables downstream DP selection to address sensing, fusion, and distribution architecture.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance test: (1) Worksite model generated within 15 minutes of site entry; (2) Terrain elevation accuracy ±0.5 m over 90% of mapped area; (3) Obstacle detection latency <5 s for static features, <2 s for dynamic hazards; (4) Model available to planning and execution subsystems via agreed interface; (5) Safety-critical features (personnel, exclusion zones) flagged with confidence metadata.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain and obstacle data from onboard and/or external sensors.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Fuse multi-source sensor data into a unified worksite model representation.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Detect and classify dynamic hazards (personnel, moving equipment) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Distribute worksite model to planning, execution, and safety subsystems with appropriate latency and fidelity.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and confidence; flag uncertain or missing regions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Onboard LiDAR + camera fusion with local SLAM; periodic export to cloud for planning.
DP-B: Distributed sensor network (vehicle + fixed beacons) with edge fusion; real-time model broadcast via network link.
DP-C: Hybrid: onboard perception (LiDAR + camera) for real-time execution; tele-operator visual input + GIS baseline for planning; centralized fusion in Safety Services.
DP-D: Operator-directed manual mapping with GPS + IMU; minimal onboard sensing; model stored in vehicle local database.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid onboard perception (LiDAR + camera SLAM for real-time execution) + tele-operator visual input + GIS baseline integration, with centralized model fusion and distribution via Safety Services. This balances real-time autonomy (onboard sensors), operator situational awareness (tele-operator feed), planning fidelity (GIS baseline), and safety oversight (centralized fusion in Safety Services).

potential_sources: DP-C couples worksite modeling to teleoperation subsystem (operator visual input) and Safety Services (centralized fusion). This is acceptable because: (1) tele-operator visual input is a known stakeholder capability; (2) Safety Services is a designated safety-critical subsystem; (3) the coupling is unidirectional (model feeds safety logic, not vice versa).
Risk: If tele-operator network link fails, model fidelity degrades. Mitigation: onboard SLAM provides fallback; GIS baseline provides planning continuity.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids tight coupling to Vehicle autonomy stack by keeping model generation separate from path planning. Model is consumed by both execution (real-time) and planning (offline) via well-defined interfaces.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Onboard LiDAR + camera hardware is available on Vehicle platform.
Assumption: Tele-operator has sufficient bandwidth and latency tolerance to contribute visual input in real-time.
Assumption: GIS baseline data (terrain, known obstacles) is available for the worksite.
Risk: LiDAR occlusion in dense vegetation or rain; camera degradation in low light. Mitigation: multi-modal fusion + operator override.
Risk: Centralized fusion in Safety Services becomes a bottleneck if model update rate is high. Mitigation: edge pre-processing in Vehicle; Safety Services performs validation/enrichment only.
Risk: Model synchronization latency between onboard (real-time) and centralized (planning) versions. Mitigation: versioning and timestamp metadata.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the model update frequency requirement (e.g., 1 Hz for execution, 0.1 Hz for planning) and the acceptable latency for model availability to planning subsystems. This will inform DP refinement at the next decomposition level (sensor selection, fusion algorithm, network bandwidth).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized worksite model fusion, validation, and distribution. Receives sensor data streams (Telemetry from Vehicle), GIS baseline, and tele-operator visual input. Produces validated worksite model and distributes to planning/execution subsystems via Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces. Owns model versioning, confidence metadata, and safety-critical feature flagging.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives onboard sensor streams (LiDAR point clouds, camera frames, IMU, GPS) from Vehicle in real-time.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Distributes validated worksite model (terrain, obstacles, work zones) to planning subsystems for mission planning.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Distributes safety-critical model features (personnel, exclusion zones, dynamic hazards) with confidence metadata to execution and operator interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives GIS baseline and tele-operator visual input; distributes model updates to Vehicle and planning subsystems.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Worksite modeling is a subsystem function, not a top-level responsibility. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse the modeling logic across multiple child modules without clear ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera) and local SLAM. However, centralized fusion, GIS integration, and safety-critical validation are outside Vehicle scope. Vehicle should produce Telemetry; Safety Services should consume and fuse. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple modeling to vehicle autonomy, violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation provides operator visual input and control. It is a data source for modeling, not the owner of model fusion or distribution. Assigning IM to Teleoperation would conflate operator control with model management. Not recommended.

Safety Services is the designated safety-critical subsystem. Worksite modeling is a safety-critical function (high criticality factor). Safety Services naturally owns model fusion, validation, and distribution to ensure safety-critical features are correctly flagged and available to safety logic. Centralized ownership minimizes diffusion and ensures single source of truth for model state.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary because: (1) Worksite modeling is safety-critical (high criticality); (2) Safety Services is the designated safety-critical subsystem; (3) Centralized fusion and validation ensure model integrity and consistency; (4) Safety Services can enforce safety-critical feature flagging and confidence metadata; (5) Model distribution via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces aligns with Safety Services' role in safety oversight.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services becomes a performance bottleneck if model fusion is computationally intensive. Mitigation: Implement edge pre-processing in Vehicle (local SLAM); Safety Services performs validation and enrichment only.

Risk: Network latency between Vehicle and Safety Services delays real-time model updates. Mitigation: Vehicle maintains local model for real-time execution; Safety Services maintains centralized model for planning and safety oversight. Synchronization via versioning.

Risk: Safety Services may lack domain expertise in sensor fusion and SLAM algorithms. Mitigation: Allocate SLAM algorithm development to Vehicle subsystem; Safety Services integrates and validates outputs.

Risk: Model distribution via SafetyAlert may overload operator interfaces if update frequency is high. Mitigation: Implement model update filtering and summarization; distribute full model via Tasking (planning), safety-critical features only via SafetyAlert (operator).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Vehicle (onboard SLAM) and Safety Services (centralized fusion). Define the Telemetry interface schema to include model metadata (timestamp, confidence, coverage). Specify the Tasking and SafetyAlert message formats for model distribution. Consider a separate 'Model Manager' module within Safety Services if fusion logic becomes complex; for now, Safety Services is the appropriate IM boundary.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid onboard + centralized fusion) directly addresses all sub-FRs: (1) onboard LiDAR + camera acquire sensor data; (2) centralized fusion in Safety Services fuses multi-source data; (3) onboard SLAM detects dynamic hazards in real-time; (4) Safety Services distributes model via Tasking and SafetyAlert; (5) Safety Services validates and flags uncertain regions. DP-C is feasible, aligns with stakeholder capabilities (tele-operator, Site Manager), and balances real-time execution with planning fidelity. No decoupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Onboard SLAM logic is distributed across Vehicle (sensor drivers, local fusion) and Safety Services (centralized validation). Mitigation: Define clear interface contract (Telemetry schema) between Vehicle and Safety Services; Vehicle owns SLAM algorithm, Safety Services owns model validation.

Diffusion Risk 2: Model distribution logic is split between Safety Services (fusion) and Teleoperation (operator visual input). Mitigation: Teleoperation is a data source only; Safety Services owns distribution logic. Clear interface (Network Link) between Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Diffusion Risk 3: GIS baseline integration is external to both Vehicle and Safety Services. Mitigation: Safety Services owns GIS integration; GIS data is ingested via Network Link as a known interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the clear IM boundary with high containment (0.9) and low diffusion (0.1). All model fusion, validation, and distribution logic is owned by Safety Services. Vehicle provides sensor data (Telemetry); Teleoperation provides operator input (Network Link); Planning subsystems consume model (Tasking, SafetyAlert). No logic is duplicated across modules. Ownership is unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via integration testing: (1) Inject sensor data (Telemetry) into Safety Services; verify model generation and distribution via Tasking/SafetyAlert. (2) Verify model fidelity against ground truth (terrain, obstacles). (3) Verify real-time latency (<5 s for static, <2 s for dynamic). (4) Verify safety-critical feature flagging via SafetyAlert. (5) Verify model availability to planning subsystems. All verification points are observable and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle allocates onboard sensor acquisition and local SLAM to Telemetry interface. Safety Services allocates model fusion, validation, and distribution to Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces. Planning subsystems allocate model consumption to Tasking interface. Safety logic allocates hazard detection to SafetyAlert interface. No ambiguity in responsibility allocation.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine the Telemetry interface schema to include sensor metadata (timestamp, confidence, sensor type, coverage). This enables Safety Services to weight sensor inputs appropriately during fusion.

Revision 2: Define the Tasking message format for model distribution (e.g., grid-based terrain map, obstacle list with coordinates, work-zone polygons). This ensures planning subsystems can consume the model correctly.

Revision 3: Define the SafetyAlert message format for safety-critical features (e.g., personnel location, exclusion zone violations, dynamic hazard alerts). This ensures operator and safety logic receive actionable information.
Revision 4: Specify the model update frequency and latency requirements for both real-time execution (Vehicle) and planning (Tasking). This will inform DP refinement at the next level.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-006 is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, fusion, dynamic hazard detection, model distribution, validation). DP-C is sound and well-justified. Safety Services is the correct IM boundary with high containment and clear ownership. All gates pass. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail sensor selection, fusion algorithms, model representation, and interface specifications. Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs will refine the design and enable detailed implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain and obstacle data from onboard and/or external sensors.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Fuse multi-source sensor data into a unified worksite model representation.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Detect and classify dynamic hazards (personnel, moving equipment) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Distribute worksite model to planning, execution, and safety subsystems with appropriate latency and fidelity.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and confidence; flag uncertain or missing regions.

assumptions: Onboard LiDAR + camera hardware is available on Vehicle platform.
Tele-operator has sufficient bandwidth and latency tolerance to contribute visual input in real-time.
GIS baseline data (terrain, known obstacles) is available for the worksite.
Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time model fusion and validation.
Model update frequency of 1 Hz for execution and 0.1 Hz for planning is acceptable.
Terrain elevation accuracy of ±0.5 m is sufficient for path planning and safety.
Obstacle detection latency of <5 s for static features and <2 s for dynamic hazards is acceptable.
open_risks: LiDAR occlusion in dense vegetation or rain; camera degradation in low light. Mitigation: multi-modal fusion + operator override.
Centralized fusion in Safety Services becomes a bottleneck if model update rate is high. Mitigation: edge pre-processing in Vehicle; Safety Services performs validation/enrichment only.
Model synchronization latency between onboard (real-time) and centralized (planning) versions. Mitigation: versioning and timestamp metadata.

Network latency between Vehicle and Safety Services delays real-time model updates. Mitigation: Vehicle maintains local model for real-time execution; Safety Services maintains centralized model for planning.
Safety Services may lack domain expertise in sensor fusion and SLAM algorithms. Mitigation: Allocate SLAM algorithm development to Vehicle subsystem; Safety Services integrates and validates outputs.
evidence_needed: Sensor specifications (LiDAR range, resolution, update rate; camera resolution, frame rate).
SLAM algorithm selection and performance benchmarks (accuracy, latency, computational cost).
Network bandwidth and latency specifications for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link interfaces.
Model representation format (grid-based, feature-based, hybrid) and storage requirements.
Safety Services computational capacity and real-time processing constraints.
Tele-operator visual input format and integration with Safety Services.
GIS baseline data availability and integration process.
Acceptance test results for model fidelity, latency, and safety-critical feature flagging.
